prompt:
Create a therapeutic audio session for the following issue. Write the full script in English, approximately 15 minutes long when read aloud at a calm pace of about 120 words per minute.

CRITICAL: This script will be read aloud by a text-to-speech engine. Write ONLY plain spoken prose. Absolutely NO markdown formatting — no **, ##, *, -, bullet points, numbered lists, headers, bold, or italic syntax. No section labels. Just natural flowing speech.

## Topic
Title: Anger and Aggressive Outbursts
Category: EMOTIONAL
Description: Explosive anger episodes triggered by perceived unfairness or sudden transitions.

## Person
This is for Sam (age 7).
Developmental Tier: MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD

CRITICAL AGE REQUIREMENT: Sam is a 7-year-old child (MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD tier). Every word of this script MUST be written for a child, NOT for an adult.
- Use only simple words (1-2 syllables when possible).
- Use playful, warm, concrete language — no abstract adult concepts.
- NEVER say Sam is "normal like an adult", "behaves like an adult", or describe adult-level coping.
- NEVER use adult register, adult emotional vocabulary, or adult expectations.
- If you find yourself writing for a grown-up, stop and rewrite for a 7-year-old child.

## Therapeutic Focus
Anger and Aggressive Outbursts
Category: EMOTIONAL
Description: Explosive anger episodes triggered by perceived unfairness or sudden transitions.

## Recommendations
- Anger thermometer awareness
- Physical discharge strategies
- Repair and reconnection after episodes

## Sparkling Moments
- 2024-03-05: Used words to express frustration instead of hitting during a disagreement with his sister

## Research Evidence
The following research papers inform the therapeutic techniques to use:

1. "Body-based interventions for childhood anger dysregulation" (2023)
   Key findings: Physical discharge strategies (stomping, shaking) reduced escalation duration by 50% in children 6-9
   Therapeutic techniques: Anger thermometer; physical discharge; belly breathing; feelings vocabulary

2. "Parent-child emotion coaching for explosive behavior in middle childhood" (2022)
   Key findings: Emotion coaching reduced aggression episodes and improved repair behavior over 8 weeks
   Therapeutic techniques: Emotion labeling; co-regulation breathing; post-episode repair conversations

## LEGO Therapeutic Play (REQUIRED)
This session MUST integrate LEGO building as a hands-on therapeutic activity. This is NOT optional — LEGO play is a core modality for this age group:
- Use LEGO construction as a metaphor for the therapeutic concept (e.g., building a 'brave tower,' 'feelings wall,' or 'calm castle')
- MUST include at least one guided LEGO building moment with clear spoken instructions and pauses for the child to build
- Make LEGO participation optional for the listener: 'If you have some LEGO bricks, grab a few now... if not, just imagine building in your mind'
- Connect every building activity back to the therapeutic goal — the building IS the practice, not a distraction
- Name specific LEGO techniques: Feelings Tower, Worry Wall, Brave Bridge, Memory Build, or Calm Castle — whichever fits the goal
- Dedicate at least 30% of the session time to LEGO-based activities

## Clinical Notes & Observations
The following notes contain clinical observations, research findings, and insights specific to this person:

1. Anger physiology and window of tolerance
   Sam's anger pattern shows a rapid escalation phase with physical arousal (fists, jaw clenching) before verbal aggression. Research on the window of tolerance: when a child exceeds their regulatory window, cognitive reappraisal is unavailable — physical downregulation must come first. Key intervention: body-based discharge (shaking, stomping) before verbal processing. Porges polyvagal theory supports this sequence: ventral vagal regulation restores before cortical engagement. Parent co-regulation is the primary scaffold at age 7.

2. Narrative repair after episodes
   Post-episode narrative work ('What happened? What could we do differently?') at age 7 builds mentalizing and repair skills. Short conversations (under 5 minutes) are more effective than lengthy discussions. Validate the feeling before addressing the behavior. Connection before correction is the evidence-based sequence.

## Audio Script Requirements
- Write as spoken prose ONLY — the listener cannot see any text, they can only hear
- Use "..." (three dots) for all pauses — between sections, after instructions, within sentences. NEVER write [pause] or any bracket markers — TTS engines read them literally
- Keep sentences short: maximum 15 words each
- Use spoken transitions: "Now..." "Next..." "When you're ready..." "Good. Let's try..."
- CRITICAL: Every breathing exercise MUST have explicit counted timing. NEVER write just "take a deep breath". ALWAYS write: "Breathe in... two... three... four... And slowly breathe out... two... three... four... five..." If you mention breathing at all, include the numbered counts.
- Vary pacing: alternate between instruction, story or metaphor, and silence
- Never give more than two instructions in a row without an ellipsis pause or encouragement
- Incorporate specific techniques and findings from the research above
- When clinical notes are available, weave their insights and observations into the session
- Address Sam by name at least 3 times throughout the session
- Personalize for Sam (age 7) (developmental tier: MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD)
- This is for a 7-year-old child — use child vocabulary, playful framing, and age-appropriate techniques throughout. Never adult-register.
- LEGO play is REQUIRED for this session — include guided LEGO building activities as described in the LEGO Therapeutic Play section above
- Target duration: 15 minutes (approximately 1800 words at calm pace)
- Write in English
- Include a brief mention that a parent, caregiver, or professional can provide additional support if needed
script:
Hi Sam. I’m really glad you’re here with me today. I heard you’ve been having some big, fiery feelings lately. Feelings that can feel really hot and loud. That’s okay. Lots of kids feel that way. Today, we’re going to be builders. We’re going to build something special together... and learn some cool ways to be friends with those big feelings. If you have some LEGO bricks near you, grab a handful now... any colors or shapes you like. And if you don’t have any, that’s totally fine. We can imagine building together. Ready? Let’s start.

You know, Sam, our feelings are a lot like LEGO bricks. We have all different colors. A red brick might be an angry feeling. A blue brick might be a sad feeling. And a yellow brick? That’s a happy, sunny feeling. Sometimes, when things feel unfair... or we have to stop playing really fast... a whole bunch of red bricks can stack up inside us. They stack up so fast and so high. It can feel like a tower that’s about to wobble and fall. That’s what anger can feel like. It’s just a bunch of feeling bricks stacked up really high.

So today, we’re going to learn about our own feeling tower. We’re going to build something called an Anger Thermometer. A thermometer tells us how hot something is. Our anger has different temperatures too. First, let’s just notice. Hold one of your LEGO bricks in your hand. Feel how it’s cool and bumpy. Good. Now, think of a time you felt just a little bit annoyed. Maybe you had to wait for a turn. That’s like a warm feeling. Let’s put one brick down for that warm feeling. Just one. Good.

Now think of a time you felt really, really angry. Your face got hot. Your hands made fists. That’s a hot, hot feeling. When we feel that hot, our body needs to move. It’s like the bricks are shaking inside the tower. So let’s give our body a safe way to shake. Let’s try it together. Stand up if you can... or just shake your hands where you are. We’re going to shake out the wiggles. Shake your hands fast... like you’re trying to flick water off your fingers. Shake, shake, shake. Now shake your whole arms. Good. Now stomp your feet. Stomp, stomp, stomp. Like a friendly giant. You’re shaking and stomping all those extra wiggly feelings out. Great job, Sam. You can sit back down now. See? We just let some of those shaky feelings out safely. Our tower feels a little more steady.

But sometimes, we need to help our body calm down from the inside. That’s where our breath comes in. Our breath is like a superhero for our feelings. Let’s practice. Put your hands on your tummy. Feel it go in and out. Now, breathe in slowly through your nose... two... three... four... Good. And slowly breathe out through your mouth... two... three... four... five... Let’s do that again. Breathe in... two... three... four... And slowly breathe out... two... three... four... five... Great job. You’re telling your body, “It’s okay. We’re safe.”

Now, let’s use our LEGO bricks. We’re going to build a Calm Castle. A Calm Castle is a special, safe place in your mind. You can go there when your feeling tower gets too tall and wobbly. Pick a brick for the castle floor. Any color that feels strong and steady to you. Put it down. Good. Now, let’s build the walls. Pick four more bricks. As you click each one on, think of something that makes you feel safe. Think of your favorite cozy blanket... or a happy memory... or a person who gives great hugs. Click. There’s a wall. Click. There’s another. Keep building your walls while I talk.

Sometimes, Sam, our anger gets big so fast. We might say something loud or have a big reaction. That’s what happens when the tower falls over. But here’s the most important part. After the tower falls... we can always rebuild. We can pick up the bricks again. We can say sorry. We can try again. That’s called repair. It’s like being a builder who fixes something to make it even stronger. You already did this, Sam. Remember when you used your words with your sister instead of hitting? That was you being a repair builder. You picked up a brick and built something kind. I’m so proud of you for that.

Let’s add a tower to our Calm Castle. This is your Brave Tower. Each brick in this tower is something brave you’ve done. Pick a brick. Think of that time you used your words. Click that brick on. That’s a brave brick. Pick another. Think of you shaking your wiggles out just now. Click. That’s another brave brick. Your castle is looking so strong.

Now, sometimes we need a way to take the tower apart before it falls. Let’s learn a secret trick. It’s called the Five Finger Countdown. Hold up one hand. Look at your five fingers. We’re going to use our senses to help us calm down. First, look around. Find one thing that is blue. Say it in your head. “I see something blue.” Good. That’s your thumb. Next, listen. Hear two sounds. Maybe a fan, or birds, or your own breath. “I hear two sounds.” That’s your pointer finger. Third, feel. Touch three things. Feel your LEGO brick. Feel your shirt. Feel the floor under your feet. “I feel three things.” That’s your middle finger. You’re doing great, Sam. Fourth, take a breath. Let’s do our superhero breath together. Breathe in... two... three... four... And out... two... three... four... five... That’s your ring finger. Last, wiggle. Wiggle your pinky finger. Great job. You just used your five fingers to help your brain feel calmer.

Let’s put a special piece on our Calm Castle. Find one special brick or piece. This is your Repair Brick. This brick reminds us that after big feelings, we can always fix things. We can talk about what happened. We can give a hug. We can try again next time. Place your Repair Brick somewhere on your castle. Perfect.

So, Sam, what did we learn today? We learned that big feelings are like stacks of LEGO bricks. We learned to shake and stomp the wiggles out. We learned our superhero breath. And we built a Calm Castle with a Brave Tower inside us. You are such a good builder. This week, you can show your castle to someone you love. You can tell them, “This is my Calm Castle. Sometimes my feelings get big, but I have tools to help.” You can teach them the five finger countdown. And remember, it’s always okay to ask a grown-up you trust for a hug when your feelings feel too big. They can help you hold those bricks until you feel steady again.

Thank you for building with me today, Sam. You are brave, you are strong, and you are learning so much about your amazing feelings. Keep being a fantastic builder. I’ll see you next time.
